advisory.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an `_acquire_fcntl` test injecting OSError(EACCES) asserting it waits then returns -1 (contention path), covering the second contention errno in the branch condition.
evidence: Source condition: `if exc.errno not in (errno.EAGAIN, errno.EACCES):` (src/rebar/_store/lock.py:141)
search for `EACCES` in tests/unit/test_lock_stale_reclaim.py returned no matches
Ticket acceptance criterion names both: "EAGAIN/EACCES: retries until the deadline, then returns -1"
ABSENCE: no test injects OSError(EACCES) to confirm it is waited out rather than re-raised
advisory.finding: The new contention branch treats both EAGAIN and EACCES as contention (`if exc.errno not in (errno.EAGAIN, errno.EACCES)`), but the tests only inject EAGAIN. EACCES's classification as contention (wait-then-return-`-1`, NOT re-raise) is never exercised, so a regression that dropped EACCES from the tuple — reclassifying it as a fault that re-raises — would pass every added test.
scenarios: A refactor drops EACCES from the contention tuple; EACCES faults now re-raise as OSError instead of mapping to LockTimeout, silently changing all ~12 LockTimeout handlers' behavior for that errno — undetected by the suite.
advisory.suggested_fix: Add a parametrized case (or a dedicated test) mirroring `test_acquire_fcntl_contention_waits_then_returns_minus_one` but raising `_errno.EACCES`, asserting `fd == -1`.

coaching.coaching: Add a regression test covering the EACCES contention branch in _acquire_fcntl.
coaching.subject: the EACCES contention branch in _acquire_fcntl
